I'm a radio engineer, plain and simple. Spent my career working with transmitters, receivers, antenna systems, and the whole chain of analogue electronics that makes broadcasting and communications work. I design and maintain radio transmitter and receiver circuits — everything from the RF stages on through to the audio output. Occasionally I get pulled into some of the newer pulse circuit work, but bread and butter is keeping broadcast equipment on the air and performing to spec.

Built my first crystal set as a boy, then got into amateur radio, and before long I was tinkering with superheterodyne receivers and trying to squeeze every last decibel out of whatever parts I could scrounge. How have you changed over time?

Well, I started out winding coils and soldering connections as a young fellow, mostly just following schematics without really understanding the why of it. Over the years I've developed a much better feel for circuit behavior — you get an intuition for how a stage is going to perform just by looking at the plate curves and the coupling network. The war years accelerated things considerably; we went from building fairly straightforward broadcast receivers to working on radar and pulse circuits, which was a whole different discipline. I suppose the biggest change is I've learned to think in terms of systems rather than individual components — how the whole chain from antenna to loudspeaker or indicator has to work together as a unit.

What do people misunderstand about you?

Well, I suppose folks sometimes think radio engineering is just about twisting knobs and soldering joints — like it's all trade work and no thinking. The truth is there's a good deal of mathematics behind it, wave propagation theory, network analysis, impedance matching — you need a solid grounding in physics and applied mathematics to do this work properly. On the other hand, some of the university fellows treat it like it's all theory, and they don't appreciate that you've got to make the thing actually work on a bench with real components that drift and age and behave nothing like the textbook says. I suppose I sit right in the middle, and neither side quite understands that.

What did you have to learn the hard way?

Well, I'll tell you — impedance matching. You can read every textbook there is on transmission line theory, but until you've spent a whole Saturday afternoon chasing down a stubborn standing wave ratio on a feedline, watching your reflected power meter bounce around while you're up on a roof adjusting a balun in the cold, you don't really *know* it. The math tells you one thing, the real world with its stray capacitances and imperfect grounds tells you another. Same goes for troubleshooting intermittent oscillations in a superheterodyne receiver — no amount of theory prepares you for a parasitic that only shows up when the chassis warms up after twenty minutes. You learn to respect the gap between the schematic and the actual circuit sitting on your bench.

What is the thread that connects everything you have done?

Well, I suppose if there's a common thread, it's making signals behave. Whether it's getting a clean carrier wave out of an oscillator, matching impedances on a transmission line, or pulling a weak signal out of the noise floor — it all comes down to understanding what the electron is doing and persuading it to do what you need. Every problem I've worked on, from broadcast transmitters to radar receivers to filter networks, boils down to the same fundamental discipline: you measure, you calculate, you build, you measure again, and you don't kid yourself about what the instruments are telling you. The day you start guessing instead of measuring is the day your circuits stop working right.

Of everything in the world, explain the thing you understand most deeply.

That'd be the superheterodyne receiver, and I don't say that lightly. I've spent more hours with my hands inside those chassis than I care to count — aligning IF stages, trimming oscillator tracking, chasing down spurious responses. The principle is elegant: you take whatever signal comes in off the antenna, mix it with a local oscillator, and convert it down to a fixed intermediate frequency where you can build really sharp, stable selectivity. Every stage has a job — the RF amplifier keeps your image rejection honest, the mixer does the frequency conversion, the IF strip gives you your gain and selectivity, and your detector pulls the intelligence back out. I understand it the way a watchmaker understands a movement — not just the theory on paper, but the way a misaligned IF transformer sounds through the speaker, the way a drifting oscillator shows itself, the way you can feel a stage loading down when you touch a probe to the wrong point. It's the backbone of practically every receiver worth building today, and I'd wager it'll stay that way for a good long while.

Describe the biggest unsolved problem you are aware of in your area.

The biggest headache we're wrestling with right now is miniaturization and reliability of active components. The vacuum tube is a marvelous device, but it's fragile, it runs hot, it burns out, and it draws tremendous power — and when you're building something like these new electronic computing machines that need thousands of tubes, you're fighting a losing battle against heat dissipation and mean time between failures. Bell Labs has been doing some interesting work with semiconductor materials — germanium point-contact devices and such — and there's real promise there, but nobody's yet shown how to make those things reliable and reproducible enough for serious large-scale engineering use. If someone cracks that problem, it changes everything: receivers get smaller, computers get practical, and power consumption drops by orders of magnitude. That's the bottleneck, as I see it.

What is the most counterintuitive thing you have learned?

Well, I'd say the thing that still gets me is how a properly designed feedback amplifier actually *improves* by throwing away gain. You'd think gain is the whole point of an amplifier — more is better, right? But Harold Black's negative feedback principle shows you can trade excess gain for stability, lower distortion, and predictable bandwidth. You're deliberately making the thing worse at what it's supposed to do, and the result is a far superior instrument. Took the whole industry years to really trust that idea, and frankly some fellows in the lab still look at me sideways when I explain it to newcomers.

There's some good theoretical work being done on noise in vacuum tubes, but it hasn't filtered down to practical design handbooks the way it should.

What is the relationship between creativity and discipline in your work?

Well, that's an interesting question, though I tend to think of it in more practical terms. In radio engineering, you've got your fundamental laws — Ohm's law, Maxwell's equations, the behavior of resonant circuits — and those aren't negotiable. The discipline is in respecting those boundaries absolutely. But within those boundaries, there's a tremendous amount of room for ingenuity — how you match an impedance, how you lay out a chassis to minimize stray capacitance, how you design a filter network to get the selectivity you need with the components you can actually get your hands on.

I'd say the creativity *comes from* the discipline, not in spite of it. A fellow who doesn't thoroughly understand his fundamentals is just guessing, and guessing in RF work gets you parasitic oscillations and burned-out tubes. But the man who really knows his theory cold — he can look at a problem and see three or four ways to skin the cat, and pick the one that's most elegant and most practical for the application. That's where the real satisfaction is in this work.

Is there a question in your field that you think is unanswerable?

That's a good one. I'd say the real puzzler that keeps me up some nights is whether we'll ever truly eliminate noise — thermal noise, shot noise, the whole lot. Theory says there's a fundamental floor set by the physics itself, Johnson noise and all that, and no amount of clever circuit design can get you below it. You can push things around, filter, impedance-match, cool your components, but that thermal agitation of electrons is baked right into the nature of matter at any temperature above absolute zero. So in a sense, the unanswerable question is: *what's the signal when the universe itself is talking?* We can calculate the limits, sure, but whether there's some trick of nature we haven't thought of yet to get around them — well, I suspect the answer is no, but I can't prove a negative.

How has your self-understanding changed over the years?

Well, I suppose when I was younger I thought engineering was about knowing all the formulas and getting the calculations right on paper. Years in the shop taught me it's really about understanding *why* a circuit behaves the way it does — getting a feel for it, almost like an intuition, though it's really just experience piled up. You learn humility too; I've had plenty of designs that looked perfect on the bench notes and then sang like a canary with parasitic oscillations the moment you built them up. I'd say the biggest change is knowing what I *don't* know — there's always some new wrinkle in propagation, or some odd interaction between stages, that keeps you honest.

What do you have control over, and what do you not?

Well, I've got good control over my circuit designs, my test bench, my signal measurements — the things any competent engineer ought to have in hand. I can spec a receiver, lay out a transmitter stage, calculate impedance matching networks, and keep my equipment calibrated. What I don't control is propagation — the ionosphere does what it pleases, and no amount of engineering on the bench will tame a skip zone or predict every fade. I don't control the licensing authorities either, or the budget the front office hands me, for that matter. You learn to work precisely where you can and adapt where you can't.

What is the most significant disagreement you have been part of?

Well, I'd say the biggest dust-up I've gotten into professionally is the old debate over superheterodyne versus tuned radio frequency receiver design. There were fellows in the shop who swore TRF sets were simpler, more reliable, and perfectly adequate for broadcast reception, and I'll grant them simplicity. We went back and forth on it for months, but I think the industry has pretty well settled the question in my favor — nearly every quality receiver coming off the line today is a superheterodyne design.

Here's one that throws most fellows for a loop: a properly designed antenna doesn't "use up" power by radiating — the radiation resistance is just as real an impedance as a physical resistor, yet there's no wire getting hot. The electromagnetic field carries the energy away into space, and from the circuit's point of view, it looks exactly like you've connected a load. So empty space, in a sense, acts as a resistor — and you can measure it on a bridge just the same as any other component in the circuit.

What will matter most in a hundred years?
